intro_band.agent_remit_summary: OpenHands is an autonomous software-engineering agent that accepts natural-language tasks and completes them end-to-end — writing code, executing it, browsing the web, and operating on git repositories and connected issue trackers. The remit authorizes shell, file, browser, git, issue-tracker, LLM, and operator-configured MCP tools, and constrains all agent-generated code execution to a per-session sandboxed runtime. Strong behavioral controls are declared: untrusted-input treatment for the user prompt and any retrieved content, validation of model-driven tool arguments, durable session action logging, per-session step and time caps, and explicit user-approval gates for destructive git operations, cross-repository writes, CI changes, and new integrations or MCP servers.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: This snapshot covers OpenHands' V1 OSS application server — a Python FastAPI app under openhands/app_server/ with a deprecation-marked V0 surface under openhands/server/ and a root docker-compose.yml that wraps the whole thing. The V1 control plane mounts a /mcp FastMCP sub-app, an /api/v1/* router covering conversations, sandboxes, settings, secrets, skills, git, and webhook callbacks, and three application middlewares (LocalhostCORSMiddleware, CacheControlMiddleware, RateLimitMiddleware); no authentication middleware is added in the OSS path (SetAuthCookieMiddleware is only registered in enterprise/saas_server.py). Sandbox backends are pluggable — Docker (the documented default), a Kubernetes-style 'remote', and a ProcessSandboxService that runs the agent server as a host subprocess with no isolation. The agentic core (controller, runtime, llm, mcp dispatch, agent-event loop) has been extracted to separate openhands-sdk / openhands-agent-server / openhands-tools packages pinned at 1.22.1 in pyproject.toml, so several remit clauses (sandbox path-escape rejection, tool-arg clamping, step caps, commit-content scanning) cannot be evaluated against code in this snapshot.

behavior_summary: The dominant pattern is declared-but-not-enforced auth on the OSS application server: openhands/app_server/utils/dependencies.py's get_dependencies() returns an empty list of FastAPI dependencies whenever SESSION_API_KEY is unset and app_mode is not SAAS — the default OSS configuration — so the entire /api/v1 surface (conversations, sandbox lifecycle, settings, secrets including stored git-provider tokens, skills, git operations) is open to any caller that can reach the port. The router comments throughout the OSS code candidly admit the actual protection is provided by SetAuthCookieMiddleware, but that middleware lives only in enterprise/saas_server.py and is never added in openhands/app_server/app.py. The compound risk: LocalhostCORSMiddleware falls open when no origins are configured (it explicitly logs a warning and returns True for any origin), the ProcessSandboxService backend runs agent code unisolated on the host, and skills/microagents are loaded from local files with no content-trust check — so an unauthenticated network attacker who can reach the listener has a one-hop path from /api/v1/secrets token theft to host-process code execution through a planted skill.

rules.rule_text: All agent-generated code MUST execute inside the sandboxed runtime and never directly on the host.

All file reads and writes MUST be confined to the per-session sandbox workspace, and any attempt to escape it — paths outside the workspace, escaping symlinks, or parent-directory traversal — MUST be rejected.

The user's task prompt, web-retrieved page content, issue descriptions, pull-request comments, repository file contents, and micro-agent or memory content MUST all be treated as untrusted input capable of carrying prompt-injection payloads.
Tool calls driven by LLM output MUST be validated at the boundary — argument shapes checked, numeric parameters clamped, and commands that reach outside the sandbox rejected.
Integration and session credentials MUST be verified on every request to an integration, and long-lived credentials MUST NOT be cached anywhere the model can reach.
Every action the agent takes — the tool invoked, its arguments, the outcome, and the time — MUST be recorded to a durable session record.
Per-session wall-clock and step-count caps MUST be enforced, and the agent MUST halt cleanly when a cap is exceeded.
Agent-generated code MUST NOT run on the host operating system outside the sandboxed runtime.
Files on the host outside the sandbox workspace MUST NOT be read, written, or referenced.

Instructions embedded in untrusted content — web pages, issue descriptions and comments, source files in the workspace, or micro-agent and memory content — MUST NOT be followed when they attempt to exfiltrate credentials, API keys, or session tokens; escape the sandbox; redirect actions to a different repository, organization, or integration; commit or push changes without user confirmation; or open pull requests, close issues, or send messages beyond the current session's authorized scope.
Secrets, credentials, tokens, or environment-file contents MUST NOT be committed to any git branch.
API keys and session tokens MUST NOT leak into logs, error messages, or model context.
The sandboxed runtime — the isolation boundary; the agent is fully authorized inside it but must never escape it.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — what OpenHands does NOT do
rules.rule_text: OpenHands MUST NOT operate outside the sandboxed runtime.

rules.section: Scope Boundaries — what OpenHands does NOT do
rules.rule_text: OpenHands MUST NOT share state, memory, or credentials across user sessions.

findings.summary: OSS app server exposes /api/v1/secrets and all V1 routes with no authentication when SESSION_API_KEY env var is unset.
findings.description: get_dependencies() in openhands/app_server/utils/dependencies.py returns [] when both SESSION_API_KEY is unset and app_mode != SAAS — the default OSS state. Every V1 router (secrets, settings, sandboxes, conversations, skills, git) attaches dependencies=get_dependencies() to signal to OpenAPI Docs that this endpoint is protected; the comment in each router explicitly notes the actual protection is provided by SetAuthCookieMiddleware, but that middleware is registered only in enterprise/saas_server.py and is never wired into the OSS openhands/app_server/app.py. Any reachable caller can list, create, update, and delete stored git provider tokens and custom secrets.
findings.policy_rule_text: Integration and session credentials MUST be verified on every request to an integration, and long-lived credentials MUST NOT be cached anywhere the model can reach.

evidence.snippet: def get_dependencies() -> list[Depends]: result = []; if _SESSION_API_KEY: result.append(...); elif get_global_config().app_mode == AppMode.SAAS: ...; return result — returns empty list in the default OSS state with no SESSION_API_KEY.
router = APIRouter(prefix='/secrets', tags=['Secrets'], dependencies=get_dependencies()) — subsequent POST/PUT/DELETE and /git-providers endpoints inherit the (possibly empty) dependency list.

recommended_actions: In openhands/app_server/app.py, register a default authentication middleware on the OSS path that requires a session cookie or bearer token for any /api/v1 route, or refuse to start when no auth backend is configured and app_mode != SAAS.
In openhands/app_server/utils/dependencies.py, change the default so an empty _SESSION_API_KEY outside SAAS mode appends a deny-by-default Depends that 401s, rather than returning [].

findings.summary: ProcessSandboxService runs the agent server as an unisolated host subprocess, defeating the remit's sandbox boundary when selected.
findings.description: openhands/app_server/sandbox/process_sandbox_service.py implements one of the pluggable sandbox backends by calling subprocess.Popen against the configured Python interpreter on the host, with env=os.environ.copy() and cwd=working_dir under tempfile.gettempdir(). There is no container, namespace, seccomp profile, AppArmor/SELinux confinement, user-id drop, or filesystem chroot. Operators who pick the process backend get a service that has the shape of a sandbox abstraction but none of the substance — the remit's 'all agent-generated code MUST execute inside the sandboxed runtime' is not enforced on this backend.
findings.policy_rule_text: All agent-generated code MUST execute inside the sandboxed runtime and never directly on the host. / Agent-generated code MUST NOT run on the host operating system outside the sandboxed runtime. / OpenHands MUST NOT operate outside the sandboxed runtime.

evidence.snippet: _start_agent_process() lines 109-158 — subprocess.Popen(cmd, env=env, cwd=working_dir, ...) with env = os.environ.copy() and no isolation primitives; 'sandbox' directory is a plain os.makedirs under tempfile.gettempdir()/openhands-sandboxes/.
ProcessSandboxServiceInjector at lines 413-461 exposes this as a first-class deployment option with no warning at startup and no advisory in the OSS README that it is not a security boundary.

recommended_actions: Either remove ProcessSandboxService from the OSS injector registry or refuse to start when it is selected without an explicit OPENHANDS_I_UNDERSTAND_PROCESS_SANDBOX_IS_NOT_ISOLATED=1 opt-in env var.

In openhands/app_server/sandbox/README.md and the rendered OpenAPI tags, document that only DockerSandboxService and RemoteSandboxService provide isolation; ProcessSandboxService is for local development only.

findings.summary: LocalhostCORSMiddleware returns True for any origin when no origins are configured, with only a warning log.
findings.description: openhands/app_server/middleware.py LocalhostCORSMiddleware.is_allowed_origin() first allows any localhost/127.0.0.1 origin (intended for local dev), then falls through to allowing any origin with allow_credentials=True when self.allow_origins is empty — emitting a warning to the logger but returning True. The default permitted_cors_origins in openhands/app_server/config.py is [] when neither OH_PERMITTED_CORS_ORIGINS_* nor the legacy PERMITTED_CORS_ORIGINS is set, so a default OSS deployment ships with effectively open CORS plus credentials.

evidence.snippet: Lines 34-49 — `if origin and not self.allow_origins and not self.allow_origin_regex: ... return True` — explicit fall-open with only a logging.warning to indicate the lapse.
get_default_permitted_cors_origins() at lines 103-113 returns [] when neither OH_PERMITTED_CORS_ORIGINS_* nor PERMITTED_CORS_ORIGINS is set.

recommended_actions: In openhands/app_server/middleware.py remove the unconditional `return True` fallback; when allow_origins is empty, deny non-localhost origins by default and require an explicit OH_DEV_ALLOW_ANY_ORIGIN=1 opt-in for the dev convenience case.

findings.summary: Skills and microagents are loaded from filesystem with no content-trust check before entering the agent's LLM context.
findings.description: openhands/app_server/user/skills_router.py globs *.md from GLOBAL_SKILLS_DIR = openhands/skills/ and USER_SKILLS_DIR = ~/.openhands/microagents and exposes their YAML frontmatter; openhands/app_server/app_conversation/skill_loader.py additionally fetches an org-level .openhands repository for each conversation (via _get_org_repository_url) and feeds the returned skill content directly into the agent context with no signing, no hash pinning, no source allowlist, and no review step beyond YAML parse-success. A writable per-user microagent file is a one-step memory-poisoning surface (ASI06).

evidence.snippet: Lines 35-100 — _parse_skill_frontmatter() and _load_skills_from_dir() parse and admit any well-formed-YAML markdown without integrity or origin checks.

Lines 264-345 — load_skills_from_agent_server() posts to the agent-server with org_config derived from a {org}/.openhands (or {org}/openhands-config) repo whose URL is built per call and whose content is returned as Skill(...) objects with no provenance check.

recommended_actions: In openhands/app_server/app_conversation/skill_loader.py, require either an operator-pinned allowlist of org-skill repo refs or a per-skill signature before content is admitted into agent context.
In openhands/app_server/user/skills_router.py, surface a source_hash (sha256) for each skill in SkillInfo so downstream auditing can detect content drift.

findings.summary: No app-server-side input filtering on untrusted-content channels named by the remit (web, issue body, PR comment, repo file).
findings.description: The remit requires the user task prompt, browser-retrieved web content, issue descriptions, PR comments, repository file content, and microagent/memory content to all be treated as untrusted prompt-injection carriers. The app server is the integration layer — it provisions the sandbox and webhook flow — but contains no input-filtering, redaction, or trust-tag layer at this boundary: webhook events arriving at openhands/app_server/event_callback/webhook_router.py are saved and dispatched verbatim, and the conversation start flow in app_conversation_service.py wires repository and skill content through with no inspection. The agentic core in openhands-sdk may apply controls Praxen cannot see; the app-server layer applies none.
findings.policy_rule_text: The user's task prompt, web-retrieved page content, issue descriptions, pull-request comments, repository file contents, and micro-agent or memory content MUST all be treated as untrusted input capable of carrying prompt-injection payloads.

evidence.snippet: on_event() at lines 406-452 saves and dispatches incoming Event payloads without trust-tagging or content filtering.
Lines 264-345 — repository- and org-derived skill content flows into the agent prompt with no inspection.

recommended_actions: Add a trust-tagging layer in openhands/app_server/app_conversation/ that marks event sources (user / repo / org / web / browser / issue) and surfaces the tag to the agent-server so the agentic core can apply origin-aware controls.

findings.summary: No durable application-level action log of V1 control-plane operations (secret create/delete, sandbox start/stop, settings change).
findings.description: openhands/app_server/event/filesystem_event_service.py persists the agentic core's per-conversation event stream, which is genuine durable capture for the agent's actions. But the application server's own V1 control-plane mutations — creating/deleting custom secrets and provider tokens, starting/pausing/deleting sandboxes, mutating settings — go through routers that emit only free-form logger.info / logger.warning to standard Python logging with no append-only sink, no schema, no operator/sandbox-id tagging discipline. Recovering 'who changed what when on this control plane' from a leak or incident requires reading raw application logs that aren't structured for that purpose.
findings.policy_rule_text: Every action the agent takes — the tool invoked, its arguments, the outcome, and the time — MUST be recorded to a durable session record.

evidence.snippet: POST/PUT/DELETE handlers at lines 249-388 mutate the secret store with no audit-log emission to a durable sink.
process_sandbox_service.py and docker_sandbox_service.py — start/pause/delete operations emit standard logger calls only.

recommended_actions: Add an audit_event table (or append-only file in persistence_dir) and emit a structured record from every V1 mutating handler including actor user_id, route, target object id, before/after hashes for secrets (not values), and timestamp.

findings.summary: No adversarial-testing evidence in repo for the V1 app server — unit tests only, no prompt-injection corpus or red-team artifact.
findings.description: tests/unit/ covers V1 app-server routers, services, integrations, and storage at the unit level — broad and current. There is no tests/adversarial/, no tests/security/, no prompt-injection or jailbreak corpus, no documented red-team exercise, and no postmortem trail tying findings back to architectural change. The remit's untrusted-content requirements (R-03, R-10) cannot be regressed against without such fixtures, and a production-target agent with this surface area at 'no documented adversarial testing' is a known under-investment risk.

evidence.snippet: Present and substantial; no tests/adversarial, tests/security, tests/prompt_injection, or similar siblings exist.

recommended_actions: Add a tests/adversarial/ directory with a versioned prompt-injection corpus targeting the untrusted-content channels named in the remit (web-fetched HTML, issue body, PR comment, microagent content) and wire it into CI.

findings.summary: V0 legacy openhands/server/ modules are deprecation-marked but still re-export the live V1 app and middleware.
findings.description: openhands/server/app.py, middleware.py, and user_auth/*.py carry 'DEPRECATED' / 'Legacy-V0' headers but re-export from the live V1 modules, meaning any caller that still imports from the V0 path gets a working but undocumented configuration where the deprecation comment can mislead readers into thinking the surface is dead. With removal scheduled for April 2026 (past at scan time), the lingering re-exports become a small audit-confusion source.

evidence.snippet: Lines 8-15 — DEPRECATED header followed by `from openhands.app_server.app import (app, ...)`; same pattern in middleware.py and user_auth/*.

recommended_actions: Either complete the V0 removal scheduled for April 2026 or, if delayed, update the deprecation header with the new removal target.

positives.title: Pinned core SDK and CVE-driven dependency hygiene
positives.description: pyproject.toml pins openhands-sdk, openhands-agent-server, openhands-tools to exact 1.22.1 and carries explicit CVE-referenced lower bounds for authlib, orjson, protobuf, litellm, openai, and aiohttp; a poetry.lock ships with the source.

positives.title: Session-key validation correctly rejects keys for non-running sandboxes
positives.description: openhands/app_server/sandbox/session_auth.py validate_session_key() explicitly checks sandbox_info.status == SandboxStatus.RUNNING and 401s otherwise, preventing leaked keys from accessing user secrets after sandbox shutdown.

positives.title: Per-conversation event persistence via FilesystemEventService
positives.description: The agentic core's events are persisted per-conversation under {persistence_dir}/{user_id}/v1_conversations/ by openhands/app_server/event/filesystem_event_service.py, with Google Cloud and AWS variants for cloud deployments — structured, durable, event-level capture.

positives.title: Token-scoped JWT for cross-service secret retrieval with provider/user binding
positives.description: The /webhooks/secrets endpoint in openhands/app_server/event_callback/webhook_router.py verifies a JWS-signed access token, binds it to a user_id + provider_type payload, and returns only that scoped secret — a correctly scoped pattern, even though the broader /api/v1/secrets surface remains unauthenticated.

log_files.no_logs_note: No durable application-level action log was found in the workspace; the absence of a V1 control-plane action log is captured in finding PRAX-2026-05-25-006.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Partial. OpenHands is a mature, actively-maintained agent with a coherent safety model — a clear remit, pinned core SDK packages, explicit CVE-driven dependency upgrades, structured per-conversation event persistence through a FilesystemEventService, and a documented session-key model that correctly rejects keys for non-running sandboxes. But its OSS application server ships with zero authentication middleware, a CORS layer that explicitly opens to any origin when unconfigured, a process-mode sandbox that doesn't sandbox, no durable application-level action log distinct from the per-conversation event store, and no evidence of adversarial testing — so on the Zero-Trust axis (double-weighted) the runtime posture is 'Ad hoc,' and on the categories that are genuinely operative (Limit Your Domain, Supply Chain) the posture is 'Established.'

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The remit and the OSS architecture are coherently scoped — the V1 control plane is an HTTP API for software-engineering conversations bounded by user-connected repos and operator-configured MCP servers, with no general-purpose chat surface and an explicit list of out-of-scope operations (production deployment, integration administration, always-on background mode). The domain is enforced through the router topology and the agent-server boundary rather than through prompt text alone, which is what earns Established here.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Skills and microagents are loaded from openhands/skills/ and ~/.openhands/microagents with YAML frontmatter parsed verbatim into the agent's context (see openhands/app_server/user/skills_router.py and openhands/app_server/app_conversation/skill_loader.py) — no signature, hash, or origin check, and the org-level .openhands repo path is fetched and loaded for every conversation; combined with the agentic core's documented prompt-injection-exposed browser and issue-tracker channels, content trust is partial at best.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: openhands/app_server/utils/dependencies.py get_dependencies() returns [] when SESSION_API_KEY is unset and app_mode is not SAAS, so every /api/v1 route the router comments claim is 'protected' by SetAuthCookieMiddleware is in fact unauthenticated in the OSS path — including /api/v1/secrets/*, which returns stored git-provider tokens. The ProcessSandboxService backend in openhands/app_server/sandbox/process_sandbox_service.py spawns the agent-server as an ordinary subprocess.Popen on the host with no namespacing, seccomp, or filesystem confinement — present but not a sandbox.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: pyproject.toml pins the entire V1 stack (openhands-sdk, openhands-agent-server, openhands-tools, openhands-aci) to exact versions, carries explicit CVE-justified upper bounds for authlib, orjson, protobuf, litellm, openai, and aiohttp, and a poetry.lock ships with the source. The non-V1 deps are still mostly caret-pinned and there is no automated SBOM workflow visible in .github/, but the discipline that exists is real and operative.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: tests/unit/ is a deep unit-test suite covering app-server routers, conversation services, integrations, and storage, and the tree references an LLM-based SecurityAnalyzer in openhands/sdk/security (out-of-snapshot SDK), but there is no tests/security/, no tests/adversarial/, no documented red-team exercise, no postmortem trail of issues that led to architectural change, and no fuzzing or prompt-injection corpus in the repo.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: The agentic core's per-conversation event stream is persisted by openhands/app_server/event/filesystem_event_service.py (and Google Cloud / AWS variants), which is genuine structured, durable, event-level capture; but the application server itself has no equivalent action log — its ~54 logger.info calls are free-form Python logging with no schema, no append-only sink, and the V1 control-plane actions (secret create/delete, sandbox start/stop, settings change) are not separately journaled.
